coredata.dc:title: Impact of first-life usage on second-life performance of lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lehmusto, Mika
Santasalo-Aarnio, Annukka
coredata.dc:description: The global transition to greenhouse gas emission-free energy is accelerating, with energy storage systems playing a crucial role in achieving net-zero CO₂ emissions by 2050. Electrification in transportation is driving a 10-fold increase in global EV battery demand by 2035, leading to growth in end-of-life batteries. This shift raises environmental concerns but also creates opportunities for circular economy (CE) strategies, including repurposing, reuse, and recycling. Despite its potential, the second-life battery business faces several challenges hindering widespread adoption, including uncertainty in technical condition, unpredictable performance and remaining lifespan for intended usage profile. This experimental study evaluates aging behavior of nickel cobalt aluminum (NCA) cells in second-life applications, examining capacity degradation and internal resistance evolution under 4 different usage patterns. In this study, first-life aging was induced through high-current and low-current cycling at 100% depth of discharge (DoD). Subsequently, second-life cycling was conducted using 30% DoD cycles at 0.5C and 0.25C current levels. The study shows that during second-life cycling under high-current conditions, cell capacity degrade at approximately 2% per 1,000 cycles, regardless of whether they experienced high-current or low-current cycling in first life. This indicates that first-life cycling conditions do not significantly influence degradation rates when subjected to identical conditions in second life. Interestingly, a slight capacity increase along with decreased internal resistance, was observed in the case with partial DoD cycling at low currents. Finally, measured data are used to analyze and compare 4 second-life battery business cases, with frequency control demonstrating economic potential and a 2.9-year payback period.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Second life battery
Circular economy
Battery cycling
Capacity degradation
Internal resistance

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electric vehicles battery demand by region. STEPS = Stated Policy Scenario; APS = Announced, Pledges Scenario; NZE = Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario.

ce:figure.ce:source: Figure is based in IEA [2] data.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Block chart of test equipment for first and second life cell cycle testing.
Diagrams introducing the used cycle profiles: a) 10 first-life cycles followed by capacity characterization. b) 100 second-life cycles followed by capacity and DCIR characterization.
Cell capacity degradation profiles as function of cycled capacity.
ACIR profiles based on EIS measurement data as a function of 100 cycles.
Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance spectra. Internal impedance in datasheet (BOL). FL Start = start of FL cycling; EOL= end of FL cycling; SL600 = after 600 SL cycles.
DCIR profiles of the cells based on DC current pulse and compared to ACIR profiles of the same cells.
Economic comparison of second life BESS based on 4 different application use cases.
Key characteristics of Samsung INR18650-35E cell
First life cycle characteristics for all the measured cells
Second life cycle characteristics for all the measured cells

head.ce:title: Impact of first-life usage on second-life performance of lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy and Mechanical Engineering, Research Group of Energy Conversion and Systems, Aalto University, Aalto 00076, Finland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy and Mechanical Engineering, Research Group of Energy Conversion and Systems, Aalto University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy and Mechanical Engineering, Research group of Energy conversion and systems, Aalto University, P.O. Box 16100, 00076 Aalto, Finland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The global transition to greenhouse gas emission-free energy is accelerating, with energy storage systems playing a crucial role in achieving net-zero CO₂ emissions by 2050. Electrification in transportation is driving a 10-fold increase in global EV battery demand by 2035, leading to growth in end-of-life batteries. This shift raises environmental concerns but also creates opportunities for circular economy (CE) strategies, including repurposing, reuse, and recycling. Despite its potential, the second-life battery business faces several challenges hindering widespread adoption, including uncertainty in technical condition, unpredictable performance and remaining lifespan for intended usage profile. This experimental study evaluates aging behavior of nickel cobalt aluminum (NCA) cells in second-life applications, examining capacity degradation and internal resistance evolution under 4 different usage patterns. In this study, first-life aging was induced through high-current and low-current cycling at 100% depth of discharge (DoD). Subsequently, second-life cycling was conducted using 30% DoD cycles at 0.5C and 0.25C current levels. The study shows that during second-life cycling under high-current conditions, cell capacity degrade at approximately 2% per 1,000 cycles, regardless of whether they experienced high-current or low-current cycling in first life. This indicates that first-life cycling conditions do not significantly influence degradation rates when subjected to identical conditions in second life. Interestingly, a slight capacity increase along with decreased internal resistance, was observed in the case with partial DoD cycling at low currents. Finally, measured data are used to analyze and compare 4 second-life battery business cases, with frequency control demonstrating economic potential and a 2.9-year payback period.

ce:section.ce:para: To combat global warming and achieve net-zero CO₂ emissions by 2050, energy storage systems play a crucial role. The transition to greenhouse gas (GHG) emission-free energy sources is not only a global necessity but also a dominant trend, particularly in the transportation sector. According to the International Energy Agency's (IEA) Announced Pledges Scenario, global demand for electric vehicle (EV) battery capacity is expected to increase 4-fold between 2023 and 2030. Furthermore, to align with the net-zero emissions targets by 2050, EV battery demand is expected to reach 9.1 TWh by 2035, representing a 10-fold increase over a decade [1].

This rapid expansion extends beyond passenger vehicles, as electrification accelerates across transportation sectors to meet decarbonization goals. Battery-powered solutions are being adopted in heavy-duty trucks, buses, taxis, trains, industrial machinery, and marine vessels. Fig. 1 presents IEA's projections for battery demand growth in these sectors.

As shown in Fig. 1, battery adoption in transportation is accelerating, leading to a rising number of end-of-life (EOL) batteries. EV batteries typically last 5–10 years, depending on usage and conditions. By 2030, projections estimate 600–700 GWh of batteries will reach EOL. This growing demand raises environmental concerns, particularly regarding raw material availability and waste generation, while also presenting opportunities within the circular economy (CE). CE principles aim to optimize resources use through redesign, repurposing, reusing, and recycling, minimizing environmental impact while creating economic value [3–5].

Recycling and reuse are essential strategies for managing EOL batteries. EV batteries retired at approximately 80% state-of-health (SOH) can still be repurposed for secondary applications, offering a cost-effective solution for stationary energy storage in residential, commercial, and industrial applications [6]. As the number of retired batteries transitioning to second-life applications increases, industrializing the reuse process is crucial for ensuring efficiency and quality. This process involves several key phases, including sorting, performance characterization, disassembly, refurbishment, and quality control [7].

Past research teams have focused on predicting the performance and aging of second life li-ion batteries (SLB) through modeling, simulation-based studies and literature reviews. Seger et al. [8,9] developed a mathematical model to predict SLB performance and lifespan. The exponential model includes depth of discharge (DoD%), current levels, average State of Charge (SoC%), and uncertainty factors. Accurate EOL estimation is crucial for assessing residual value in second-life applications. The model is based on literature data from nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) and lithium manganese oxide (LMO) cells. Casals et al. [10] analyzed SLB performance in 4 stationary applications using a MATLAB-based simulation model. Their findings suggest a significant battery life extension, especially in fast-charging and self-consumption scenarios, with SLB lifetimes projected to reach up to 30 years. However, the model’s accuracy in replicating real-world NMC cell cycling is uncertain and requires uncertainty margins. The MATLAB model incorporates key aging factors such as C-rate, SoC, DoD, and temperature. Shahjalal et al. [11] reviewed a broad range of SLB assessment methodologies, including both test-based and algorithmic approaches, to evaluate their applicability for SLB deployment. Their study examines degradation models addressing electrode material loss, lithium depletion, and rising internal resistance. However, it lacks detailed case studies or concrete examples, such as SLB lifetime estimates or remaining energy throughput, which are essential for practical validation. White et al. [12] compared repurposed NCA, lithium iron phosphate (LFP), NMC, LMO, and NMC-LMO EV batteries for grid energy arbitrage (EA), using a mathematical analysis model to evaluate usable energy capacity, volume, and thermal performance to generate a performance score. The study found significant performance variations, indicating that not all EV battery chemistries suit identical SLB applications. They used the same modeling methodology to rank SLB batteries for electricity grid frequency regulation (FR) services [13].

Previous research also includes experimental studies to quantify the remaining lifespan and assess the suitability of retired traction batteries for second-life use. Cicconi et al. [14] demonstrated that batteries with LFP cell chemistry can achieve an additional 1,000–1,300 cycles beyond their EOL, making them suitable for smart grid storage applications. Maheshwari et al. [15] analyzed capacity fade in commercial 18,650 NMC cells using electrochemical characterization, impedance spectroscopy, and post-mortem analysis alongside the cycling process. The study identified a slow linear fade phase with rising serial resistance and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance, followed by rapid capacity decline. The transition point correlated with anode interphase resistance growth. Operating temperature was found to have the strongest impact on cell lifespan. Agubra and Fergus [16] conducted an empirical investigation of the most common anode degradation mechanisms and their correlation with internal impedance. Ecker et al. [17] demonstrated through extensive set of accelerated cycle testing that for 18,650 NMC cell, capacity fade is minimized when cycling around 50% SoC, regardless of the cycle depth. Lacap et al. [18] conducted an experimental study on microgrids integrating photovoltaic (PV) generation and SLB storage to reduce peak power demand and energy consumption. Their findings showed a 39% reduction in peak-time energy use and a 60% drop in maximum power demand, with LMO/NMC-based SLB BESSs having the greatest impact. After 1 year, the SLBs remained reliable, with no significant capacity loss despite a 71% initial SOH, confirming their viability for microgrid applications.

Extensive research has examined the life-cycle assessment (LCA), environmental impact, and circular economic aspects of second-life batteries (SLBs), consistently concluding that reuse significantly reduces the environmental footprint compared to manufacturing new batteries. The production of an EV battery generates emissions ranging from 56 to 494 kgCO₂e/kWh [19]. For a 50 kWh battery pack, this translates to approximately 2,800 to 24,700 kg of CO₂ emissions, which can be mitigated through repurposing for suitable applications. Cusenza et al. [20] reviewed 8 LCA studies and found that the global warming potential (GWP) of battery production varies between 52.6 and 487 kgCO₂e/kWh, which aligns well with previous estimates. Ahmadi et al. [21] used an LCA model to evaluate the reuse of retired EV batteries in stationary applications, reporting a 56% reduction in CO₂ emissions when repurposed batteries were used to meet peak electricity demand, compared to power generation from natural gas during peak demand. Similarly, Ioakimidis et al. [22] conducted LCA scenario analysis and demonstrated that repurposing EV batteries for secondary applications offers substantial environmental benefits compared to manufacturing new batteries for the same purpose. Ahmadi et al. [23] extended the LCA framework to assess both first and SLB use, concluding that cascaded battery utilization provides significant advantages across most environmental impact categories compared to conventional single-use systems. In conclusion, existing research strongly supports the environmental benefits of SLB applications, showing significant reductions in CO₂ emissions and reinforcing the role of battery reuse in sustainable energy systems.

The economic viability of SLBs has been widely analyzed in techno-economic studies, assessing their cost-effectiveness compared to new battery storage and energy systems without storage. Song et al. [24] examined the financial benefits of integrating SLBs into wind power systems to mitigate intermittency. Their study incorporated battery degradation and profit models, comparing EOL batteries with new ones. Case studies in the USA and Denmark revealed that, under current wind energy and battery price conditions, SLBs are not yet profitable. However, they may become competitive if wind energy prices fall faster than battery costs. Fallah and Fitzpatrick [3] analyzed the economic potential of EV SLBs for grid services such as frequency reserve and energy trading in Ireland. By modeling battery aging to estimate the remaining service life, their study found that non-energy services yield the highest benefit-cost ratio, whereas energy trading provides higher cash flow and shorter ROI, favoring short-term investments. Colarullo and Thakur [25] developed a techno-economic model for SLB integration in local energy communities, showing that SLBs increase energy self-sufficiency by 3% and reduce payback time to 5 years compared to new batteries. However, limited lifespan constrains economic viability, and the impact of time-based tariffs remains unexamined. If SLB prices fall below 150 €/kWh, their economic feasibility surpasses PV-only scenarios. Bhatt et al. [4] evaluated SLB feasibility in a microgrid, comparing off-grid and grid-connected systems with SLBs and new batteries. Findings indicate that grid-connected SLBs reduce net capital costs and energy costs by 36% and 35%, respectively. However, lifespan assumptions remain unclear. Horesh et al. [26] assessed reconditioned battery economics, testing 3 reconditioning methods with resale prices between $34/kWh and $62/kWh. However, the study provides limited insight into SLB performance. Živčák et al. [5] examined the market potential for SLB, identifying 2 revenue strategies for regulatory energy services. Estimated SLB prices for 2020, 2025, and 2030 were €30, €21, and €14.5 per kWh, respectively. Both strategies proved profitable within specific utilization conditions. Tsiropoulos et al. [27] projected future battery price trends, finding that used EV battery packs are resold at approximately 50% of new battery costs. SLBs could further reduce stationary storage costs by 20%, equating to 30–45 €/kWh savings. Martinez-Laserna et al. [28] reported SLB prices ranging from $44 to $180 per kWh, noting that repurposing retired batteries can reduce upfront costs by up to 25%. Montes et al. [29] developed a 3-stage framework assessing state of health, technical feasibility, and economic viability for transitioning batteries to second-life applications. The National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL), in collaboration with the U.S. Department of Energy, created a cost estimation calculator for SLB repurposing, considering facility, personnel, and operational costs [30]. SLB economic benefits in the maritime industry have also been analyzed in the literature [31]. In conclusion, research confirms clear economic opportunities for SLBs. However, to ensure profitability, business developers must consider usage profiles, volumes, case-specific constraints, and local cost elements carefully, as well as conduct detailed sensitivity analysis.

Understanding and addressing safety aspects in second-life cell usage is critical for effective risk assessment. This issue has been examined in the research literature, notably by Coron et al. [32] who concluded that lithium plating and increased internal resistance are key risks to SLB safety. Some cells remain viable despite plating, provided robust thermal management and resistance monitoring are implemented. However, the lower thermal runaway onset temperature must be carefully considered in battery management to ensure safe second-life operation.

The performance and longevity of retired batteries, along with life cycle assessments and economic opportunities, have been widely studied. However, experimental, laboratory-based studies remain scarce compared to modeling approaches and literature reviews, particularly for NCA cells. This study addresses that gap by presenting novel experimental data on battery aging behavior across 4 distinct usage patterns. Specifically, we examine how first-life usage profiles influence second-life performance and longevity, providing new insights into real-world degradation mechanisms.

Our analysis evaluates cell capacity degradation rates under high- and low-current conditions with partial DoD cycles, alongside the evolution of internal resistance across different cycling profiles using both AC and DC measurement methods. By offering a deeper understanding of NCA battery aging dynamics in second-life applications, this study provides critical reference data for modeling efforts and economic feasibility assessments. Importantly, it delivers empirical evidence on the usability, remaining lifespan, and residual value of retired EV batteries in low-demand stationary storage applications, providing practical insights for both researchers and industry stakeholders.

A key objective of this study is to investigate how different first-life cycling patterns affect cell aging and second-life performance. This study employs the traditional EOL criterion for lithium-ion batteries, as defined in 1996 by the United States Advanced Battery Consortium (USABC), which defines EOL as when delivered capacity falls below 80% of the rated capacity [33]. While this definition remains widely used, its applicability is increasingly debated due to variations in the behavior of different lithium-ion chemistries and cell loadings. To reach the EOL SoH level of approximately 80%, half of the cells were cycled using high current (HC), and the other half with low current (LC). Following first-life cycling, second-life cycles were also conducted using high and LC conditions for comparative analysis.

The cells were divided into 4 groups, each containing 3 cells, based on their cycling conditions: HC-HC, HC-LC, LC-HC, and LC-LC. Due to inherent variability in battery behavior, the 2 most similar cells in each group were selected for detailed analysis.

Research process, case setup, research questions, and goals: The study aims to determine whether using significantly lower currents during first-life cycling affects the remaining life of retired (EOL) batteries, as compared to high-current cycling. Specifically, we compare low-current (LC: 0.58 C discharge/0.29 C charge) cycling with high-current (HC: 1.18 C discharge/0.58 C charge) cycling. Cells were divided into these 2 groups for first-life cycling, which was conducted at 100% DoD. The experimental setup is detailed in Table 2 on page 9. Notably, HC cycling during the first life imposed greater stress on the cells, as the maximum temperature limit was frequently reached. A protection algorithm was regularly activated to prevent the cells from exceeding the thermal threshold.

In the next phase, the second-life cycling profile is intentionally less stressful, utilizing lower currents and a reduced DoD to simulate charge-discharge patterns typical of renewable energy applications. In all cases, the DoD was limited to 30%, centered around a 50% state of charge (SoC). The low-current profile uses 0.25 C, while the high-current profile uses 0.5 C.

The primary research questions and goals are as follows:
a)
To assess the impact of varying levels of first-life cycling stress on the remaining cycle life during second-life applications.
b)
To evaluate the influence of different second-life cycling currents (0.25 C vs. 0.5 C) on the remaining cycle life of NCA cells.
c)
To estimate the number of remaining cycles based on the observed degradation patterns across different usage profiles.
d)
To quantify the residual economic potential based on the estimated remaining cycles, with further analysis presented in Chapter 3.4.
The research methods are detailed in the following chapters.

The cell type selected for this research is the Samsung INR18650-35E, a lithium-ion rechargeable cell [34]. This cell features a nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA, LiNiCoAlO₂) cathode and a graphite anode, which is a widely used combination in lithium-ion battery technology. The NCA chemistry is well-regarded for its high energy density, making it particularly suitable for applications that demand extended runtime, such as EVs and portable electronic devices. The main properties of the battery are presented in Table 1.

The cycling test environment is composed of several interconnected components designed for precise testing and monitoring of the cells under investigation. The setup includes a control PC, a cell cycle tester (Arbin BT2000), and an auxiliary electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tester (Camry), which is integrated with and managed by the cycle tester. The cell tester acts as the primary actuator, facilitating cell connection to the electrical circuit, power feed for charging, power drain for discharging, as well as measurements of voltage, current, internal resistance, charge/discharge capacity, and energy. Each cell under test is connected to the test channels via a wire harness, enabling seamless data acquisition. Temperature measurements for each cell are captured using thermocouples, ensuring accurate thermal monitoring throughout the tests. The entire setup of twelve cells under test is housed within a fume cupboard (VISU) to ensure safety. The system configuration and its components are illustrated in Fig. 2.

As depicted in Fig. 2, each cell is connected to the tester channels using 5 wires. The cells are securely housed in custom-designed holders for 18,650 cylindrical cells, featuring welded joints and 4-probe contacts to ensure precise electrical connections. The power cables for the positive and negative terminals are thicker to minimize resistance and are bolted to the cell holder cables. Thinner analog signal wires are used to measure the voltage directly from the cell terminals. A thermocouple is attached to the center of each cell to enable continuous temperature monitoring.

Test programs were developed in the tester control application (Arbin’s MITS Pro), running on the control PC, with 7 distinct programs tailored for this research: HC and LC cycling for first-life and second-life cycle tests, along with characterization programs to validate capacity, DC internal resistance (DCIR), and AC internal resistance (ACIR). The control PC also enabled program initiation and monitoring via Arbin’s Data Watcher. Upon test completion, results were exported as CSV files for further analysis and archiving.

All tests were conducted within a temperature-controlled and insulated steel container. Second-life cycling tests were performed continuously (24/7), with characterization measurements taken periodically after every 50 or 100 cycles.

For this study, we used cells that were retired from a mobility application and remained suitable for research purposes. Specifically, the tested cells were removed from electric bicycle battery packs but had not yet reached traditional EOL capacity levels. At the beginning of the research, the SoH of the cells ranged from 87% to 91%. The first step of the study involved cycling these cells to induce degradation until they approached or fell below the traditional EOL SoH threshold of 80%. This phase, referred to as "first-life cycling," aimed to simulate the natural aging process of the cells under controlled conditions. The cycling protocol consisted of 100% DoD cycles followed by constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) charging cycles. The objective was to continue this cycling process until the desired SoH degradation level was reached.

A key aspect of the study was to evaluate whether the current level during the first-life cycle, either HC or LC, significantly influenced the cells' second-life performance. This would simulate the difference between harsh and light usage of a mobile application. To address this, 12 cells were divided into 2 groups: 6 were cycled with HC and 6 with LC, as summarized in Table 2.

From Table 2, it is evident that the discharge and charge currents for the HC cells were double those used in the LC test cases. At the completion of the first-life cycling, the SoH of the cells ranged from 74% to 82%, with an average SoH of 78% and a median value of 79%.

The first life cycling with HC was designed to push the performance limits, effectively simulating a demanding usage profile. The charge current was set at the maximum specified in the datasheet, while the discharge current was 4 A, positioned between the standard 3.4 A and the stated maximum of 8 A. During the end phase of the discharge, the 4 A current caused a temperature rise close to the allowable limit. To prevent exceeding the maximum temperature, discharge breaks were implemented for thermal management. Approaching the maximum temperature clearly indicates that the profile represents a high-stress scenario for the cell. Fig. 3 illustrates the first-life cycle profile with 100% DoD and the second-life cycling batch diagram, consisting of 100 cycles at 30% DoD.

It is well-established in battery research that exposure to the lowest and highest charge levels accelerates cell aging. To mitigate this effect, the second-life cycling in this study focused on avoiding the extremes of the voltage range by using 30% DoD cycles. The primary objective was to investigate the aging behavior under partial cycling conditions and, secondarily, to assess the impact of current levels during these partial cycles.

Two current levels were tested: HC at 0.5 C (1.7 A) and LC at 0.25 C (0.85 A). To ensure comparability, the cells were divided into 4 groups based on their SoH following the first-life cycling, with a maximum variation of 3 percentage points within each group.
Six cells were cycled with HC and 6 with LC during the second-life cycling process, and their cycling details are presented in Table 3.

As shown in Table 3, the test cases are categorized into 4 groups, each comprising 3 cells: HC-HC, HC-LC, LC-HC, and LC-LC. Fig. 3b illustrates the second-life cycling batch diagram, consisting of 100 cycles at 30% DoD. Each batch is followed by a characterization phase, including capacity and DC internal resistance measurement.

After each cycling batch, both in first-life and second-life cycling phases, the standard characterization test case set was conducted to detect potential changes compared to the status after previous batches. The detailed parameters for capacity, DCIR, and ACIR measurement are provided in the following sections. The temperature setpoint for both cycling and characterization was room temperature (20°C), although some variation was observed during testing.

The capacity measurement was conducted using a cell cycler, employing a CC-CV charging profile. The cell was charged to 4.2 V, corresponding to a 100% SoC, and then discharged to 2.65 V (0% SoC), at which point the test cycle was terminated. The constant current (CC) charging phase used a current of 0.5 C (1.7 A), with a cut-off current of 0.02 C (68 mA) during the constant voltage (CV) phase. Discharging was performed at a current of 0.2 C (680 mA), in accordance with the datasheet from the manufacturer. The manufacturer specifies 1 C as equivalent to 3400 mA.

The DCIR measurement procedure begins with the cell at 0% SoC, where the initial DCIR sample is recorded. The measurement was conducted using a 1 A pulse lasting 60 ms. Following this, the cell is charged at 0.5 C (1.7 A) for 22 minutes, after which it undergoes a rest period. Once the rest period is complete, the next DCIR measurement is performed. This cycle was repeated until the cell reaches 4.0 V, with the final samples taken at that point. The SoC range measured extends up to approximately 84%–85%.

ACIR measurement was derived from EIS, which determines the real and imaginary components of a cell's internal resistance over a specified frequency range. In this study, a sinusoidal signal with an amplitude of 1 A was applied over a frequency range of 0–2000 Hz, yielding 45 sample points per test run for each cell. Each sample includes measurements of real impedance (Zreal), imaginary impedance (Zimg), impedance modulus (Zmod), and phase angle (Zphz) at the corresponding frequency. For the purposes of comparison, the ACIR value was defined as the Zmod value at 1000 Hz. In a Nyquist diagram [34,35] the ACIR value is typically identified at the point where the real axis is intersected, and the imaginary component of impedance is at its minimum. EIS measurements are commonly utilized for various purposes, including analyzing degradation mechanisms, characterizing battery performance, parametrizing and validating models, and estimating the state of life [36,37].
This section introduces the methods used to evaluate the potential financial benefits of SLBs in 4 distinct use cases: FR, power fee optimization, EA, and solar energy storage.

All economic estimations are based on a 2 MWh BESS constructed from cylindrical 18,650 NCA battery cells, with a remaining capacity of 80% of their rated capacity. A total of 204,248 cells are required to construct the 2 MWh/1 MW SLB. The assumed price for a new cell is €2.55. According to a review of the literature [6], the price of SLB cells is estimated to have a 30%–70% cost advantage compared to new cells. Hence, in this study, we assume that the overall SLB battery system is valued at 50% of their original cost, and with a sensitivity analysis performed for the range of 30%–70%. In this analysis, the price of a new BESS is assumed to range between 400 and 450 €/kWh [38]. In the following analysis, we use the SLB BESS market price for investment calculations, which includes the actual EOL BESS cost as well as all refurbishment-related expenses, such as inspection, standardized testing, maintenance, repair, classification, and quality assurance. It is important to note that prices are constantly evolving due to market dynamics, technological and supply chain factors.

For each introduced potential use case, the payback time is estimated according to Equation (1), where the total initial investment includes the cost of the SLB energy storage system and installation expenses. The mean annual cash flow is calculated as the difference between the annual revenue minus the combined annual trading fee and operations and maintenance (O&M) costs. The base estimate for the SLB BESS price assumes a residual value of 50% of the cost of a new battery. The installation cost is estimated to be 10% of the BESS price. The trading fee is assumed to be 20% of the total revenue, while the fixed operations and maintenance (FOM) cost is set at 3.5% of the BESS price, in alignment with literature values that suggest 2.5% per kilowatt [38]:
(3) Payback Time (years) = (SLB BESS cost + Installation cost)/Mean Annual Cash Flow

As defined in Eq. (2), the revenue from frequency containment reserve (FCR) services is determined based on the number of FCR bids (each with a bid power 1 MW and duration of 1 hour) over the course of a year (8,760 bids) and 2024 the average FCR-N bid price set by the national grid operator, reported in the literature as 25.39 €/MW,h [39]:
(2) FCR Revenue (€) = Annual Number of Bids ⋅ Average Bid Price (€)

As defined in Eq. (3), EA revenue is generated by leveraging dynamic electricity contracts, purchasing electricity when prices are relatively low, such as during nighttime, and selling it during daily peak hours when tariffs are high. In this scenario, we assume 5,000 cycles with 30% DoD, each involving 600 kWh, over a period of 10 years. Installation and maintenance costs are estimated in the same manner as in the frequency reserve use case. The sales price of 0.44 €/kWh is derived from market data [40], while the purchase price, which includes energy costs, distribution fees, and electricity taxes, is assumed to be 0.08 €/kWh. The price difference can be utilized to enhance profit margins for EV charging sessions or, alternatively, the stored energy can be sold back to the electricity provider:
(3) EA Revenue (€) = (Electricity price (€/kWh) – Electricity cost (€/kWh)) ⋅ Cycles ⋅ DoD(kWh)

Excess solar PV production can be utilized to charge the BESS, enabling stored energy to be used for applications such as EV charging during nighttime. In this calculation, we assume an electricity cost of 0.154 €/kWh aligned with market information, including 0.087 €/kWh [41] for electricity and electricity tax, and 0.067 €/kWh for distribution. This scenario eliminates the need to draw energy from the grid for EV charging, thereby optimizing self-consumption of solar energy. In the economic analysis, we will vary the purchase cost of SLB BESS and assess its impact on the payback period.

Power level-based costs can be optimized using SLB BESS during both the investment and operational phases. In this analysis, we have considered a medium-voltage grid connection cost of 42 €/kW [42] and a monthly power fee of 4.32 €/kW, based on market information [43].

This experimental analysis examines cell aging through 4 distinct use case scenarios, focusing on capacity degradation and changes in DC and AC internal resistance. The final objective is to quantify the economic potential of repurposing these battery systems and modules.

The studied NCA cells were first utilized in real EV applications during their first life. In the HC group, the first-life operation in an EV resulted in a SoH decline to between 88.17% and 90.27%. The aging process continued on a cycle tester, where an additional degradation of 11.26%–13.89% was observed. For the low-current (LC) group, the first-life EV operation ended with an SOH range of 87.09%–90.27%, followed by additional cycling on a tester, further reducing SoH by 7.16%–12.09%. As described in Chapter 2.3, the HC cells experienced a significantly more demanding first-life cycle due to higher current loads. In contrast, the LC cells were cycled with current levels set at 50% of those used for HC cells, ensuring a less aggressive aging profile.

The second-life cycling consisted of 600 cycles, each with a 30% DoD, resulting in a total throughput of approximately 620 Ah per cell. During these cycles, the HC cells were subjected to twice the current levels of the LC cells. The capacity evolution throughout the cycling process is presented in Fig. 4.

As illustrated in Fig. 4, diagrams HC-HC and LC-HC, during second-life cycling, under HC conditions, cells exhibit a degradation rate of approximately 2% per 1,000 cycles, regardless of whether their first-life usage involved HC or LC cycling conditions. The type of first-life cycling appears to have no significant effect on the degradation rate during second-life cycling when the same second-life cycling conditions are applied. Based on the trendlines for both cases, the degradation from 80% to 70% SOH requires approximately 5,000 cycles at 30% DoD under high-current conditions.

As shown in Fig. 4, during second-life cycling under LC conditions, HC-LC cells maintained at 75% ± 2% SOH exhibit an average capacity degradation rate of 0.6% per 1,000 cycles. With this trend, degradation from 80% to 70% SOH level is projected to require over 10,000 cycles at 30% DoD under LC conditions. An interesting result is observed in the LC-LC case, where cells with SOH close to 80%, unlike other test cases, exhibit an average capacity increase of 0.8% per 1,000 cycles.

This result has at least 2 notable practical implications. First, it suggests that if a BESS has degraded to 80% SOH, the charging strategy during its first life, whether predominantly slow overnight charging or frequent high-current fast charging, does not significantly impact capacity degradation or the expected lifetime in a second-life application.

Second, the findings indicate that when subjected to partial cycles, specifically 30% DoD with LC levels, the aging during second-life use remains minimal. This observation aligns with previous research for NMC-LMO energy storage [18]. The result of linear degradation after 80% with partial DoD and low C-rates is also aligned with the findings of previous research [44]. Low second-life degradation at low cycling currents has also been reported in previous research on NMC811 cells [45]. The result in the LC-LC case also suggests that when both first-life and second-life cycling conditions are relatively non-stressful (0.6C–0.3C), the degradation rate can be significantly lower compared to the other test case categories and the remaining lifespan notably longer.

After every 100-cycle test batch, ACIR measurements were conducted as part of the characterization test case set. ACIR serves as a key diagnostic tool, allowing assessment of the state of health and overall aging progression of the studied cell.

In the early stages of battery life, ACIR typically remains stable, as the cell operates within its designed parameters and degradation mechanisms are minimal. However, as the battery undergoes aging, ACIR gradually increases due to a variety of degradation processes. These include electrolyte decomposition, which leads to the formation of a thicker SEI layer on the anode, lithium plating, the loss of active material in both electrodes and the electrolyte, and electrode degradation, resulting in reduced electronic conductivity.

In the later stages of the cell life cycle, ACIR often increases more rapidly due to the cumulative effects of these degradation mechanisms. The primary contributors to this accelerated rise include increased SEI layer resistance, electrolyte depletion, and mechanical damage to the electrodes, such as cracks or delamination [46].
Fig. 5 presents a comparative analysis of ACIR behavior across the different studied use cases, illustrating how internal resistance evolves over time under varying conditions.

As observed from the ACIR profiles in Fig. 5, the second-life high-current cases (HC-HC and LC-HC) exhibit minimal ACIR degradation, with rates ranging from 1 mΩ to 4 mΩ per 1,000 cycles across the test scenarios. In contrast, when second-life cycling is conducted at low current (HC-LC), AC impedance degradation is more pronounced, reaching 8 mΩ per 1,000 cycles, which corresponds to approximately 15% decrease.

Interestingly, the highest AC impedance degradation is observed in the LC-LC test case, where cells experience LC in both first-life and second-life. In this scenario, the degradation rate increases to 15 mΩ per 1,000 cycles, equating to a 21% decrease in ACIR. Additionally, ACIR values in the LC-LC test case are consistently higher than in the other test cases throughout the measurement period.

At this stage of the battery’s second life, the overall ACIR behavior remains relatively stable, particularly when AC impedance reaches approximately 50 mΩ, suggesting that degradation is slowing down.

This result indicates that aging mechanisms such as electrolyte decomposition, SEI layer formation, and lithium plating have not yet significantly contributed to a notable increase in internal resistance during second-life cycling. This indicates that the cell retains substantial cycle life potential, making it well-suited for continued use in partial DoD second-life applications.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) provides valuable insights into the electrochemical reactions and aging mechanisms occurring within the battery cell. In the following analysis, we compare the EIS results across different test case categories, examining key points in the cell life cycle. These include the initial impedance profile at the start of first-life cycling (FL Start), the profile recorded when the cell reaches EOL, and the post-test results after 600 second-life partial cycles (SL600). The evolution of the EIS spectra for the selected cells is illustrated in Fig. 6, highlighting changes in internal impedance and reaction kinetics over time.

As observed from the EIS profiles in Fig. 6, the internal impedance, determined by the ACIR values at the x-axis crossing, exhibits notable changes throughout the battery’s lifecycle. The original rated internal impedance at the beginning of life was 35 mΩ, meaning that, on average, the HC-cycled cells experienced a 9 mΩ impedance increase during their use in an EV application before FL cycling in the laboratory. During the first-life (FL) HC cycling, the ACIR of cell 2 increased by 14.9 mΩ, while the ACIR of cell 9 increased by 10.9 mΩ from the start of first-life cycling (FL Start) to EOL. This indicates that FL cycling contributed 3.7 mΩ more to internal resistance growth than the preceding EV usage.

For the LC cycled cell 11, the ACIR increased by 13.3 mΩ during its period as an EV battery before FL cycling began. During FL LC cycling, impedance increased by 5.9 mΩ from FL Start to EOL. This suggests that LC cycling exerted lower stress on the cell compared to HC cycling. Second-life HC cycling does not cause any clear changes to the internal impedance of this cell.

Interestingly, cell 10 exhibited a significantly higher ACIR increase of 19.3 mΩ during its initial EV usage period. Furthermore, the data also indicates that between FL Start and EOL, the ACIR increased surprisingly by 19.8 mΩ, even though cycling was of the low-current type. This is potentially due to differences in the cell’s electrochemical characteristics compared to other cells. After FL measurements were completed and second-life cycling with LC cycles began, the first characterization round after 50 second-life cycles showed that the internal resistance had decreased by 5.9 mΩ. Overall, after 600 second-life LC cycles, the internal resistance had decreased by 13.2 mΩ. Over the entire lifespan, the final value was only 5.7 mΩ higher than the average of the 12 cells. The observed "healing" effect is likely caused by the reduced stress on the cell and a combination of electrochemical reactions, such as SEI restructuring, electrolyte redistribution that improves ionic pathways, and possible lithium redistribution.

When evaluating the aging of first-life HC-cycled cells, which exhibited an average impedance increase of 12.9 mΩ, reaching the 56–58 mΩ range by EOL, their behavior remained highly consistent in EIS measurements. Similarly, the LC-cycled cell 11 demonstrated stable performance, reaching an impedance of 54 mΩ—slightly lower than that of the HC-cycled cells, as expected. Ultimately, after the "healing" process, the LC-LC cycled cell 10 returned to an impedance level of 61 mΩ, showing that the final impedance values across the cells remained relatively close to one another. This consistency in results, as also observed in the analysis of Fig. 5, suggests that the cells retain significant potential for partial-cycle second-life applications.

Existing research indicates minimal internal resistance increase over 10,000 second-life cycles when utilizing a downscaled voltage range in [47]. Additionally, studies suggest that adopting a mild load profile, including reduced charging rate and limited voltage range, can serve as a strategy to extend the lifespan of lithium-ion cells [44].

After every 100-cycle test batch, DC internal resistance (DCIR) measurements were conducted as part of the characterization test case set. DCIR serves as a key parameter to assess both the health status and the overall aging progression of the studied cell. As a battery ages, DCIR gradually increases, signaling the potential presence of various degradation mechanisms. Unlike AC impedance, which is measured across different frequencies in EIS measurements, DC impedance evolution focuses on resistance changes under a steady-state condition or as in this test, a pulse load condition.

The increase in DC internal resistance, also referred to as ohmic resistance, can result from multiple root causes. One contributing factor is electrolyte decomposition, which leads to a reduction in ionic conductivity. Another root cause is the continuous growth of the SEI layer, which decreases the accessible surface area for lithium-ion transport. Additionally, changes in electrode porosity due to volume expansion, as well as current collector corrosion, can further contribute to the rise in DCIR [48]. These degradation mechanisms not only result in increased impedance but also lead to power fade, affecting overall cell performance.

Next, we examined the evolution of DC internal resistance throughout the entire cycling test and compared the observed trend with the corresponding ACIR measurements, which represent the real part of impedance. The results are presented in Fig. 7, illustrating the comparative behavior of DCIR and ACIR over the cycling period.

As observed in the diagrams of Fig. 7, DCIR remains largely stable during partial DoD cycling in the HC-HC, LC-HC, and HC-LC test cases. However, in the LC-LC scenario, a notable decrease of approximately 10 mΩ in DCIR can be observed. It is also worth noting that in this particular test case, the overall resistance level is consistently higher compared to the other 3 cases.

Additionally, a clear correlation between DCIR and ACIR measurements can be observed across all test conditions. This strong consistency validates the reliability of the measurement methodology, confirming its feasibility and the absence of significant anomalies or inconsistencies in the recorded data. Existing research shows similar results when comparing direct current pulse resistance with alternating current resistance measurements [49].

The stable resistance behavior observed during partial-cycle testing, along with the low-capacity degradation rate, indicates the potential for extended cycle life and high energy throughput in SLB applications. This suggests that such batteries are particularly well-suited for operational scenarios dominated by partial cycles, where performance demands do not require maximum power output or full capacity utilization.

In this analysis, 4 SLB use cases are evaluated, focusing on economic aspects and comparing their feasibility under identical conditions with a 2 MWh capacity and 1 MW power capability. The objective is to assess, based on current cost levels, the potential revenue streams and expected payback periods for each application. The evaluated SLB ESS use cases include frequency control, EA, solar PV integration, and power boosting.

As the SLB market remains in its early stages, the price of second-life batteries [6] varies significantly, introducing both a substantial business risk and, depending on market conditions, a potential opportunity. Furthermore, revenue models for different energy services exhibit considerable variability, contributing to financial uncertainty. The results of this comparative analysis are presented in Fig. 8.

From Fig. 8, it can be observed that the median investment and its variation remain consistent across all cases. The base investment cost is calculated under the assumption that the SLB ESS, including installation costs, amount to 50% of the price of a new battery. The variation range reflects scenarios where the ESS cost is either 30% or 70% lower than that of a new battery. Investment cost plays a crucial role in determining the payback period and its associated variability. Moreover, payback time is strongly influenced by annual revenue, which is highest for the frequency control application and lowest for the power booster case.

The frequency control service operates by bidding a 1 MWh capacity for both upward and downward FR. In an optimal scenario, the full reserve capacity of the ESS can be offered for each hour of the year. The revenue of 222 k€ is derived from the average bid price in 2024. From this revenue, a trading fee of 20% and a fixed operational and maintenance (FOM) cost of 3.5% of the battery purchase price are deducted. The payback time calculation suggests a notably favorable return on investment, with an estimated payback period of 2.9 years and a variation range from −1.2 to +1.4 years.

In the EA use case, the primary distinguishing factor is the revenue potential. Revenue is generated from the price differential between the standard EV charging energy sales price of 0.44 €/kWh and the lower nighttime electricity purchase price of 0.08 €/kWh. While sales prices remain relatively stable throughout the week, electricity can be stored in the ESS during the lowest-cost hours of the day. Based on the estimation in chapter 3.1, where the SLB is estimated to achieve 5,000 cycles at 30% DOD (600 kWh) over a 10-year period, the annual revenue amounts to approximately 108 k€. This results in a payback period of 5.1 years, with a variation range between −2.2 and +2.6 years, indicating a relatively positive economic outlook for this application.

In the solar PV use case, the key distinction from previous cases lies in the revenue generation mechanism. Here, it is assumed that a residential or small-scale commercial customer occasionally produces more energy than needed and can store the excess electricity in the SLB ESS for later use in EV charging. By utilizing stored energy, the user avoids drawing power from their low-voltage grid connection, which operates under a long-term fixed-price electricity contract [37]. This contract includes a total electricity cost of 15.4 c/kWh, encompassing the network fee and electricity tax. Based on the estimation in Chapter 3.1, where the SLB is projected to complete 5,000 cycles at 30% DOD (600 kWh) over a 10-year period, the resulting annual savings amount to approximately 46 k€. This corresponds to an SLB ESS payback period of 15.4 years. Given that the projected payback time most likely exceeds the expected lifetime of the SLB ESS, this investment is unlikely to be financially viable if BESS is dedicated solely to PV energy storage services.

In the power booster use case, the SLB ESS is utilized to reduce the power rating of the grid connection contract and to minimize the maximum monthly power fee by peak shaving, where the SLB ESS supplies power during periods of high demand. Fig. 8 illustrates that by incorporating the ESS, the total investment costs are reduced by 42 k€, achieved by utilizing 1 MW of BESS power for peak shaving, thereby decreasing the required grid investment by 1 MW ⋅ 42 €/kW [42]. Additionally, annual savings from peak shaving are derived from the monthly power fee of 4.32 €/kW [43] for 1000 kW, resulting in an annual cost reduction of 52 k€. This leads to a payback period of 11.9 years. The economic viability of this use case depends significantly on the price of the SLB ESS. If the cost of an SLB ESS is approximately 30–40% of the price of a new BESS, the payback period could be reduced to less than 9 years, making the investment commercially more viable. Furthermore, if multiple applications, such as solar PV and power booster described earlier, are integrated into the same BESS, the combined payback period is estimated at 5.3 years, with a variation range between −2.6 and +2.9 years, indicating a relatively favorable economic outlook for the combined use case.

These estimations highlight clear business opportunities in reusing retired EV batteries for second-life applications. However, they also indicate that payback periods may be excessively long if the business case is not well-planned upfront. Additionally, the selection and understanding of SLB technology are crucial, as battery pack aging is influenced by multiple factors, including battery chemistry, manufacturing quality, battery management system (BMS) intelligence, pack design, and auxiliary systems. Furthermore, usage patterns, operating conditions, storage conditions, and calendar aging significantly impact both new and SLB longevity. Usage patterns, extensively tested for NCA cells in this study, play a critical role in determining the remaining lifetime of SLBs. The findings suggest that careful planning of discharge and charge currents, DoD, average state of charge (SoC), operating temperature, voltage range, and daily cycled energy is essential to accurately simulate and validate aging rates and forecast remaining lifetime. This, in turn, enables a more reliable analysis of business potential.

For an accurate business case calculation, it is essential to develop a detailed cost model that accounts for the cost breakdown and local (national and regional) price variations across key cost elements, including materials and services, both fixed and variable costs, taxes, and fees. However, even a well-structured model carries inherent uncertainties and risks, making it crucial to perform sensitivity analyses to assess the impact of potential fluctuations in key factors. Critical variables include the future price trajectory of SLBs, the volatility of frequency reserve compensation in the coming months and years, electricity price evolution, advancements in automated battery pack diagnostics and SoH analysis, and cost reductions driven by the accelerated industrialization of the SLB sector. Failure to anticipate these shifts could lead to miscalculations in expected returns, highlighting the need for robust risk mitigation strategies in financial modeling.

Our analysis aims to evaluate cell capacity degradation rates under HC and LC conditions with partial DoD cycles, alongside the evolution of internal resistance across different cycling profiles using both AC and DC measurement methods. The analysis compares aging differences resulting from different first-life usage patterns, providing insight into their long-term effects on second-life performance. By enhancing the understanding of NCA battery aging dynamics in second-life applications, this study serves as critical reference data for modeling efforts and economic feasibility assessments. Importantly, it provides experimental evidence on the usability, remaining lifespan, and residual value of retired EV batteries in low-demand stationary storage applications, offering valuable insights for both researchers and industry stakeholders.

This study demonstrates that during second-life cycling under HC conditions, cells degrade at approximately 2% per 1,000 cycles, regardless of whether they experienced HC or LC cycling in first life. This indicates that first-life cycling conditions do not significantly affect degradation rates when subjected to identical HC conditions in second life. The trendlines suggest that under 30% DoD, the SOH decline from 80% to 70% requires approximately 5,000 cycles under HC conditions.

Under low-current (cell group: HC-LC) second-life cycling, capacity degradation is 0.6% per 1,000 cycles, projecting that the 80%–70% SOH decline would require over 10,000 cycles at 30% DoD. A notable exception is observed in the LC-LC case, where cells with an initial SOH close to 80% surprisingly exhibit an average capacity increase of 0.8% per 1,000 cycles. However, AC impedance degradation in this case is more pronounced, reaching 8 mΩ per 1,000 cycles, corresponding to a 15% reduction. This effect is likely due to reduced cycling stress from the partial DoD usage profile, facilitating SEI restructuring, electrolyte redistribution, and possible lithium redistribution.

First-life HC-cycled cells exhibited a consistent impedance increase of 12.9 mΩ, stabilizing at 56–58 mΩ, while LC-cycled cell 11 reached 54 mΩ. Following the observed "healing" process, LC-LC cycled cell 10 stabilized at 61 mΩ. This overall consistency across cell groups highlights their potential for second-life applications using partial cycling.

From an economic perspective, frequency control and EA present a strong business case, with estimated payback times of 2.9 years and 5.1 years, respectively. In contrast, solar PV and power booster applications alone appear less feasible due to extended payback periods exceeding 10 years. However, integrating these applications, solar PV and power booster, within the same BESS, improves feasibility, reducing the combined payback period to 5.3 years.

This study demonstrates that lithium-ion cells can be successfully repurposed for second-life applications, particularly those with moderate performance requirements. Additionally, second-life batteries present economic opportunities due to their significantly lower cost compared to new batteries. Beyond economic benefits, battery reuse also delivers substantial environmental advantages, as each repurposed EV battery reduces CO₂-equivalent emissions by several thousands to tens of thousands of kilograms, significantly lowering the carbon footprint compared to manufacturing new batteries.

To support the development of a CE for retired batteries, further research and development are required to accurately assess performance and remaining lifespan for intended second-life applications. A streamlined and cost-efficient repurposing process is essential to ensure safe, reliable, and commercially viable resale. The SLB industry has the potential for significant growth but achieving this requires industrial-scale efficiency to minimize processing time and costs while maintaining high quality and reliability. Advancing this sector demands further research in standardization, common design principles, battery health assessment, aging prediction, supply chain logistics, mechanical handling, and automation of electrical testing. In a broader sense, collaborative efforts from the entire battery ecosystem—including designers, manufacturers, distributors, users, owners, regulators, and authorities—are essential to accelerating adoption and ensuring sustainability.
Conceptualization & Methodology & Visualization & Analysis & Writing-original draft: M.L. Methodology & Review & editing & Supervision: A.S.A.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere gratitude to BSc Ella Koivisto and MSc Aravindda Venkatesh for their invaluable assistance in the measurement and data collection processes. Additionally, special thanks go to MSc Jesse Pirhonen, Manager of the Autonomy and Mobility Laboratory, for his essential support in constructing the cycling test laboratory, setting up the equipment, and providing Python programming guidance for sensor data mining.

ce:section.ce:para: Data will be made available upon request.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Driving to the future of energy storage: techno-economic analysis of a novel method to recondition second life electric vehicle batteries
Appl. Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Horesh et al., “Driving to the future of energy storage: Techno-economic analysis of a novel method to recondition second life electric vehicle batteries,” Appl Energy, vol. 295, Aug. 2021, doi: 10.1016/j.apenergy.2021.117007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Li-ion batteries for mobility and stationary storage applications: scenarios for costs and market growth
Luxembourg

sb:publisher.sb:name: Publications Office of the European Union

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Tsiropoulos, D. Tarvydas, and N. Lebedeva, Li-ion batteries for mobility and stationary storage applications: scenarios for costs and market growth. Luxembourg: Publications Office of the European Union, 2018. doi: 10.2760/87175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery second life: Hype, hope or reality? A critical review of the state of the art

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Martinez-Laserna et al., “Battery second life: Hype, hope or reality? A critical review of the state of the art,” Oct. 01, 2018, Elsevier Ltd. doi:10.1016/j.rser.2018.04.035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Procedure for assessing the suitability of battery second life applications after EV first life
Batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Montes, M. Etxandi-Santolaya, J. Eichman, V.J. Ferreira, L. Trilla, and C. Corchero, “Procedure for Assessing the Suitability of Battery Second Life Applications after EV First Life,” Batteries, vol. 8, no. 9, Sep. 2022, doi: 10.3390/batteries8090122.

J. Power Sources

J. Electrochem Soc.

J. Energy Storage

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-energy li-ion cells: impact of electrode ageing on second life viability
J. Electrochem Soc.

J. Power Sources

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Second-life lithium-ion battery aging dataset based on grid storage cycling
Data Brief.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Moy, M.A. Khan, S. Fasolato, G. Pozzato, A. Allam, and S. Onori, “Second-life lithium-ion battery aging dataset based on grid storage cycling,” Data Brief, vol. 57, Dec. 2024, doi:10.1016/j.dib.2024.111046.

ce:glossary-entry.ce:glossary-def: Alternating current internal resistance, at 1 kHz
Measure of the rate at which battery is being charged or discharged (1 C=100%/hour)
Depth of discharge [%]. Discharge energy amount compared to capacity of battery.
